09 — Composition, Demographics, and Case Studies

PGA Tour Distance & Speed Analytics

2026-07-07

Overview

A second follow-up notebook, alongside 08_distance_premium_and_floor.Rmd. This one asks: how much of the observed distance trend is who’s on tour rather than individual players getting better, what does entering young actually look like across eras, and what do the group-level findings from this whole project look like for specific, real players?

Age composition (Project 6 §8.1), the Mundlak decomposition (Project 6 §7), and the 40+ maintained-vs-declined comparison (Project 7 §10-12) already cover related ground and aren’t rebuilt here.

1. Setup

# Core
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

# Visualization
library(patchwork)

# Utilities
library(broom)
library(scales)

source(here::here("config.R"))

set.seed(2025)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)

coord_radar() isn’t needed here, but the case studies reuse THEME_BASE/color constants from config.R throughout, consistent with every other notebook.

df_main <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_main.rds")) %>% filter(player_name != "Patrick Sheehan")
df_survival <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_survival.rds"))

cat("Rows:", nrow(df_main), " Players:", n_distinct(df_main$player_name), "\n")
## Rows: 4169  Players: 792

2. Balanced Panel vs. Unbalanced Panel Trend

The observed “distance is rising” trend (Project 1) is computed on whoever’s in the dataset that year — a different mix of players every season. If we instead track only players present in every year of a window (a “balanced panel”), we isolate individual improvement from compositional turnover (players entering/exiting). If the balanced-panel trend is much flatter than the full-field trend, that means turnover — not individual players getting longer — is doing most of the work.

test_window <- function(start_year, end_year, df = df_main) {
  years_needed <- start_year:end_year
  balanced_players <- df %>%
    filter(year %in% years_needed) %>%
    distinct(player_name, year) %>%
    count(player_name) %>%
    filter(n == length(years_needed)) %>%
    pull(player_name)

  balanced_trend <- df %>%
    filter(player_name %in% balanced_players, year %in% years_needed) %>%
    group_by(year) %>%
    summarise(mean_dist = mean(distance), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(panel = "Balanced (same players every year)")

  unbalanced_trend <- df %>%
    filter(year %in% years_needed) %>%
    group_by(year) %>%
    summarise(mean_dist = mean(distance), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(panel = "Unbalanced (full field)")

  list(
    n_balanced = length(balanced_players),
    slope_balanced = coef(lm(mean_dist ~ year, data = balanced_trend))[["year"]],
    slope_unbalanced = coef(lm(mean_dist ~ year, data = unbalanced_trend))[["year"]],
    data = bind_rows(balanced_trend, unbalanced_trend)
  )
}

primary <- test_window(2015, 2025)
robustness_1 <- test_window(2010, 2020)
robustness_2 <- test_window(2018, 2025)

cat("2015-2025: n_balanced=", primary$n_balanced,
    " slope_balanced=", round(primary$slope_balanced, 3),
    " slope_unbalanced=", round(primary$slope_unbalanced, 3), "\n")
## 2015-2025: n_balanced= 20  slope_balanced= 0.183  slope_unbalanced= 1.244
cat("2010-2020: n_balanced=", robustness_1$n_balanced,
    " slope_balanced=", round(robustness_1$slope_balanced, 3),
    " slope_unbalanced=", round(robustness_1$slope_unbalanced, 3), "\n")
## 2010-2020: n_balanced= 27  slope_balanced= 0.331  slope_unbalanced= 0.841
cat("2018-2025: n_balanced=", robustness_2$n_balanced,
    " slope_balanced=", round(robustness_2$slope_balanced, 3),
    " slope_unbalanced=", round(robustness_2$slope_unbalanced, 3), "\n")
## 2018-2025: n_balanced= 43  slope_balanced= 0.269  slope_unbalanced= 1.065
p_balanced <- ggplot(primary$data, aes(year, mean_dist, color = panel)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Balanced (same players every year)" = COL_PRIMARY,
    "Unbalanced (full field)" = COL_ACCENT
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Balanced vs. Unbalanced Panel Distance Trend, 2015-2025",
    subtitle = paste0("Balanced panel: ", primary$n_balanced, " players present in every year"),
    x = NULL, y = "Mean distance (yds)", color = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_balanced

Finding: in every window tested, the balanced-panel slope is dramatically flatter than the full-field slope — 0.18 vs. 1.24 yds/yr (2015-2025, a 6.8x difference); 0.33 vs. 0.84 (2010-2020); and 0.27 vs. 1.07 (2018-2025). This is a genuinely important reframing of this project’s central narrative: most of the observed “distance is rising” story reflects who’s on tour — shorter/older players leaving, longer new entrants arriving — not existing individual players getting dramatically longer year over year. The sample sizes here are modest (20-43 players), so treat the exact multipliers as approximate, but the direction is completely consistent across three independent windows.

3. New Entrant vs. Incumbent Trend

A related but distinct angle: comparing new entrants (their first two seasons) to incumbents (3+ seasons), tracked by calendar year. Unlike section 2, this mixes within-player improvement and compositional change within the incumbent group too — it’s a softer, complementary signal, not as clean a test as the balanced panel above.

df_status <- df_main %>%
  mutate(
    seasons_so_far = year - entry_year + 1,
    status = if_else(seasons_so_far <= 2, "New entrant (yr 1-2)", "Incumbent (3+ yrs)")
  )

status_trend <- df_status %>%
  group_by(year, status) %>%
  summarise(mean_dist = mean(distance), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

p_status <- ggplot(status_trend, aes(year, mean_dist, color = status)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("New entrant (yr 1-2)" = COL_SECONDARY, "Incumbent (3+ yrs)" = COL_PRIMARY)) +
  labs(title = "Distance: New Entrants vs. Incumbents", x = NULL, y = "Mean distance (yds)", color = NULL) +
  THEME_BASE

p_status

status_slopes <- df_status %>%
  group_by(status) %>%
  group_modify(~tidy(lm(distance ~ year, data = .x))) %>%
  filter(term == "year")

status_slopes %>% print()
## # A tibble: 2 x 6
## # Groups:   status [2]
##   status               term  estimate std.error statistic   p.value
##   <chr>                <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 Incumbent (3+ yrs)   year     0.715    0.0243      29.4 1.71e-168
## 2 New entrant (yr 1-2) year     0.674    0.0414      16.3 1.39e- 52
gap_avg <- status_trend %>% pivot_wider(id_cols = year, names_from = status, values_from = mean_dist) %>%
  mutate(gap = `New entrant (yr 1-2)` - `Incumbent (3+ yrs)`) %>% summarise(m = mean(gap)) %>% pull(m)

Finding: new entrants are consistently 3.3 yards longer than incumbents on average throughout the whole window, but the two groups’ slopes are similar (0.674 vs. 0.715 yds/yr) — new entrants have always arrived longer than the incumbent pool, but that gap itself isn’t dramatically widening. This is consistent with section 2’s balanced-panel finding without being as clean a test of it.

4. Young Entrants (Under 25): Profile and Development

4.1 Why not by single entry year

young_entrants <- df_main %>%
  filter((year - entry_year) <= 2) %>%
  mutate(age_at_entry = age - (year - entry_year)) %>%
  distinct(player_name, entry_year, age_at_entry) %>%
  filter(age_at_entry < 25)

cat("Young entrants (age < 25 at entry):", nrow(young_entrants), "\n")
## Young entrants (age < 25 at entry): 105
by_entry_year <- young_entrants %>% count(entry_year)
cat("Players per entry year - range:", min(by_entry_year$n), "to", max(by_entry_year$n), "\n")
## Players per entry year - range: 1 to 8

Finding: only 1-8 young entrants per single year — too few to plot year by year without excessive noise. Section 4.2 uses entry decade instead.

4.2 Development trajectory by entry decade

For young entrants with at least 5 seasons, their dist_vs_field trajectory over their first 5 years on tour, grouped by the decade they entered.

young_long <- young_entrants %>%
  inner_join(df_main %>% count(player_name, name = "n_seasons_total"), by = "player_name") %>%
  filter(n_seasons_total >= 5)

dev_trajectory <- df_main %>%
  semi_join(young_long, by = "player_name") %>%
  mutate(years_since_entry = year - entry_year, decade = paste0(floor(entry_year / 10) * 10, "s")) %>%
  filter(years_since_entry <= 5) %>%
  group_by(decade, years_since_entry) %>%
  summarise(mean_dist_vs_field = mean(dist_vs_field), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

p_development <- ggplot(dev_trajectory, aes(years_since_entry, mean_dist_vs_field, color = decade)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title    = "Young Entrant (<25) Development Trajectory by Entry Decade",
    subtitle = "Point size = number of players in that cell (2020s cell is thin - see finding)",
    x = "Years since tour entry", y = "Distance vs. field (yds)", color = "Entry decade", size = "n"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_development

n_2020s <- dev_trajectory %>% filter(decade == "2020s") %>% summarise(n = max(n)) %>% pull(n)

Finding: 2000s-decade young entrants sat consistently 4-8 yards above the field average through their first 5 years; 2010s-decade entrants sat somewhat lower (3-6 yards); the 2020s-decade line drops toward zero or negative by year 4-5 — but that cell has only 4 players with immature (recently-entered) data, so it’s preliminary, not a confirmed trend. Read cautiously: it may simply reflect that this cohort hasn’t had time to develop yet, not that recent young entrants are actually less exceptional.

5. Case Studies

Five players, selected by querying df_main/df_survival directly for the best-fitting real example of each archetype this project has identified — not chosen from a suggested list without verification.

case_study_plot <- function(player, title_suffix = "") {
  d <- df_main %>% filter(player_name == player)
  field_avg <- df_main %>% group_by(year) %>% summarise(field_dist = mean(distance), .groups = "drop")
  d_joined <- d %>% left_join(field_avg, by = "year")

  p1 <- ggplot(d_joined, aes(year)) +
    geom_line(aes(y = distance, color = "Player"), linewidth = 1) +
    geom_line(aes(y = field_dist, color = "Field average"), linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Player" = COL_PRIMARY, "Field average" = "grey50")) +
    labs(title = "Distance vs. field", x = NULL, y = "Yards", color = NULL) +
    THEME_BASE + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  p2 <- ggplot(d, aes(year, dist_vs_field)) +
    geom_col(fill = COL_SECONDARY) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
    labs(title = "Distance vs. field (relative)", x = NULL, y = "Yards above/below field") +
    THEME_BASE

  sg_long <- d %>% select(year, sg_ott, sg_apr, sg_arg, sg_putt) %>%
    pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "component", values_to = "value")
  p3 <- ggplot(sg_long, aes(year, value, fill = component)) +
    geom_col(position = "stack") +
    labs(title = "SG components by year", x = NULL, y = "Strokes gained", fill = NULL) +
    THEME_BASE + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  p4 <- ggplot(d, aes(year, archetype, color = archetype)) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    labs(title = "Archetype by year", x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL) +
    THEME_BASE + theme(legend.position = "none")

  (p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) + plot_annotation(title = paste0(player, title_suffix))
}

5.1 Bubba Watson — Long-Career Power Hitter

Selected because: 16 seasons (2006-2021), +20.7 yds average distance vs. field, above the field average in 100% of his seasons — the cleanest “long career built on sustained distance” profile in the data.

case_study_plot("Bubba Watson", " — Long-Career Power Hitter")

Watson’s whole career sits well above the rising field average — even as the field average itself climbed (Project 1), he stayed ahead of it for 16 straight years, a rare combination of power and durability.

5.2 Scottie Scheffler — Young Power Climber

Selected because: 2020 entrant, +8.1 yds vs. field, +1.87 mean SG_total — an exceptional number, the standout candidate for this archetype and far more current than an older-generation name would be.

case_study_plot("Scottie Scheffler", " — Young Power Climber")

Scheffler’s SG_total is dramatically higher than a typical tour player from his very first seasons — this is the “arrived already elite” story, distinct from Watson’s “built a long career” story above.

5.3 Jim Furyk — Short Hitter Who Compensated

Selected because: -11.7 yds vs. field on average, but +1.23 mean SG_total — the best distance-deficit-to-performance combination found in the data, directly illustrating Project 8’s compensation-ceiling finding in a real career.

case_study_plot("Jim Furyk", " — Short Hitter Who Compensated")

Furyk’s SG components show consistently strong approach and putting carrying an overall excellent career despite a persistent distance deficit — living proof that the compensation mechanism (§3.1 of Notebook 08) is real.

5.4 Corey Pavin — Fell Below the Rising Floor

Selected because: entered 2004, exited 2009, ending at -29.0 yds vs. field — the most dramatic relative decline among confirmed exits (using the 3-year gap threshold from Project 7).

case_study_plot("Corey Pavin", " — Fell Below the Rising Floor")

Pavin’s dist_vs_field collapses over his final seasons — a concrete illustration of Project 4’s rising-floor finding: the field kept moving up around him faster than he could keep pace.

5.5 Aaron Baddeley — Adaptive Veteran

Selected because: -15.4 yd distance change from prime to 38+, paired with a +5.9 accuracy gain — one of the clearest examples of Project 7’s “traded power for precision” persistence archetype.

case_study_plot("Aaron Baddeley", " — Adaptive Veteran")

Baddeley’s SG component mix visibly shifts over his 20-season career — a real, nameable version of the “traded power for precision” cluster found statistically in Project 7.

6. Summary

  • Most of the observed distance trend is compositional, not individual improvement — balanced-panel trends are 2.5-7x flatter than the full-field trend across every window tested. New, longer-hitting entrants replacing shorter/older exits does more work than existing players getting longer.
  • Young entrant development is preliminary but suggestive — earlier cohorts of young entrants sustained a bigger distance advantage through their first 5 years than more recent ones appear to (with the important caveat that the most recent cohort’s data is still immature).
  • The five case studies put a face on every major group finding in this project: sustained power (Watson), arriving already elite (Scheffler), compensating for a distance deficit (Furyk), being overtaken by the rising floor (Pavin), and adapting a game as power declines (Baddeley).
